part: The White Negroes of Kelly Settlement
title: Born in the Kelly Settlement
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop and Guha Shankar at the University of Southern Mississippi in December 2015, Vernon F. Dahmer Jr. opens by placing his birth in the Kelly Settlement five miles north of Hattiesburg in August 1929, and names his father, Vernon Dahmer Sr., the youngest of twelve children born to George Dahmer and Ellie Kelly.

title: The Daughters Sent North
summary: Dahmer explains that his grandfather, fearing the danger white men posed to his six daughters in Mississippi, sent them north at a young age, where they married Black men, while the light-skinned sons except his father moved to Chicago and assimilated as white men in search of better employment.

title: They Looked Like Whites
topic: Passing and Comfort in Black Identity
summary: He reflects that the assimilated uncles stayed in touch and sometimes brought white wives to visit, that the family's light complexion traced back to slavery and racial mixing, and that he himself, raised in an all-Black community and married to a dark-skinned woman, never felt drawn to pass.

title: Where All the White Negroes Live
summary: Dahmer traces his grandfather George Dahmer, whose father was the German Peter Dahmer of Darmstadt and whose mother Laura Barnes was raised by a mulatto woman running a boarding house in Covington County, then married the ex-slave Charlie Craft, with whom she had nine mixed-race children, and explains why the Kelly Settlement, home to the Kelly, Wilson, and Eaton families, accepted them, a community known by the slur as the place where all the white Negroes lived.

title: Several Hundred Acres
summary: Dahmer describes his grandfather George as a successful farmer of several hundred acres who fed twelve children off the land, and his father, given a start in land after marrying, becoming a landowner who in the mid-1930s bought a school bus to carry Black children to their segregated school.

title: A Tractor and a Grocery Store
summary: He recalls his father leasing the bus to the county and hauling people to conventions and games, then using the wartime economic upturn around Camp Shelby to buy the neighborhood's first tractor in 1940 and open a grocery store around 1942, expanding the farm and serving Black and some white customers.

title: Hauling Three Hundred Pound Blocks of Ice
topic: The Ice House and a Boy's Chores
summary: Dahmer describes the country grocery store stocked with canned goods, the homemade ice house his father built beside it, and his own job at fourteen of driving the school bus to buy three-hundred-pound blocks of ice, delivering them, and chipping them into smaller blocks for customers without electricity.

title: Three Areas of Income
summary: He recounts learning to cut meat and butcher hogs in the grocery business, the row of sons providing cheap labor for room, board, and weekend spending money, and his father adding a small sawmill around 1945 to sell lumber to local farmers, giving the family three streams of income.

title: Three Sets of Kids
summary: Dahmer explains the family's three sets of children: three by his mother, who divorced his father around 1935; three by a second wife who died of a muscular disease; and the existing mother his father married in 1952, and describes his father keeping the two older boys himself rather than placing them with grandparents after the divorce.

title: We All Danced by the Same Music
topic: A Father Who Was the Focal Point
summary: He describes being raised largely without a mother as older women came to cook and clean, his father meeting the need for both parents while working, and the unusual unity and bonding this produced among five sons who all followed one direction and remained close into adulthood.

part: Founded on a Church and a School
title: Warren Kelly Gave the Land
summary: Dahmer credits his great-grandfather Warren Kelly, son of slave master Green Kelly and an African mistress named Sarah, with using his land to build the first church and school and structure the community, the first school opening in 1879 after earlier classes met in the Shady Grove Baptist Church, established in 1864.

title: A Church and a School Out of Slavery
summary: He recounts that Black members left the white Providence Baptist Church to found Shady Grove in a one-room log cabin that also served as the first school, taught by family members from the 1870s, and that his grandfather George had to attend Jackson State to win permission to marry the educated Ellen Kelly.

part: Schooling Half a Mile From Home
title: Not Sending You to Carry a Lunch
summary: Dahmer describes the country school half a mile from his home as a big family reunion where everyone knew one another, his father's saying that he was not sent to school to carry a lunch since work waited at home, and the steady loss of classmates to fieldwork that left only eight in his graduating class.

title: He Bumped Me Upside the Head
summary: He recalls favoring math and reading and struggling with grammar, and how his father, watching him misspell words at the board when picking up the bus, drilled him at home by the kerosene lamp, rapping his head for each missed word until he learned to break words into syllables.

title: A Little Thin Green Black History Book
summary: Dahmer describes a progressive principal who taught African American history in the 1940s, the thin green book on Booker T. Washington, George Washington Carver, and the Underground Railroad used in seventh and eighth grade, and the hand-me-down white-school textbooks with pages missing and other children's names inside.

part: An Open Heart at the Store
title: Poor White Folks Picking Cotton
topic: Race and Custom at the Store
summary: He recalls extremely poor white people who came to pick cotton in segments of the field, and white customers who charged groceries and sometimes walked off owing his father, including a returning veteran who left several hundred dollars unpaid, as Dahmer worked the store cutting meat, selling ice, and pumping gas.

title: A Drink and a Moon Pie
summary: Dahmer describes his father giving poor Black families who walked in from the woods a drink and a moon pie and extra meat, and traces that open heart to George Dahmer, who treated his sharecroppers as family in a communal arrangement Dahmer links to the interdependence that came out of slavery.

title: To My Own Self, Not on Bad
summary: He recounts buying back, for nine hundred fifty dollars, the inherited land of a nineteen-year-old who had let his taxes lapse, setting the young man on a payment plan and teaching him to pay taxes and claim the homestead exemption, work the young man now does cutting sugar cane and mowing yards for the family.

title: Tougaloo and Alcorn
summary: After noting a Claiborne County man who sold land cheaply in what became Jones Village, Dahmer describes enrolling at Tougaloo, falling seriously ill his first semester, then attending Alcorn State, leaving to help the family business when his stepmother fell ill and died, and returning until the draft ended his schooling with thirty-eight credits earned.

title: Counting Crickets
summary: He recalls Alcorn State as a remote agricultural school with a work program, strict rules forbidding any contact when walking a girl to the dormitory, and his comfort there among rural farming students, majoring in agriculture and working in the school dairy, and notes Tougaloo had white faculty teaching Black students.

part: You Got to Be Twice as Good
title: Twice as Good to Stay Out of Trouble
summary: Dahmer says his father was never registered to vote and was conscious of being denied the same opportunity as whites, never speaking meanly of them but telling his boys they had to be twice as good and work twice as hard because it was not even, counsel that steadied him later in the Air Force.

title: News On, You Be Quiet
topic: A Household That Followed the News
summary: He describes his father drawing power from the school bus battery to run a small RCA radio, requiring the family to sit quietly for John Cameron Swayze's evening news alongside Lum and Abner, Amos and Andy, and the Grand Ole Opry, and his parable about the hog eating acorns without looking up to see where they come from.

title: Nine Black Men and a Secret Branch
summary: Dahmer recounts his father and eight other Black men founding the county branch of the NAACP around 1945 as an underground organization with secret night meetings, his father reporting back what was said about voting and politics, and taking the teenaged Dahmer along to pay the poll tax at the sheriff's office.

title: Tell Me What You Read
summary: He describes watching the white registrar hand his father a card to read aloud, then declare what his father said was wrong, denying registration to a business owner who had helped Dahmer with his own lessons, a denial that aggravated the boy and that he later connected to his father becoming NAACP president in the mid-1950s after Brown v. Board.

title: You Mean Mister Son
summary: Dahmer recounts how, home as an Air Force staff sergeant around 1955, the rising racial tension showed in small things, a store owner's wife correcting him to call her teenage son Mister and a grocer quizzing him on whether integrated Nebraska had ruined him, and how his father warned him on visits home to stay away from windows at night and be aware of a gun kept in the room.

title: Picking Up Rocks at San Antonio
summary: Dahmer recounts being drafted during the Korean War, traveling by Trailways bus to Jackson and lodging on Farish Street because there was no hotel for the white recruits, then induction and weeks at a San Antonio Air Force base sleeping in tents and moving rocks from pile to pile before basic training in Wichita Falls.

title: Don't Get Caught Up in This Army Thing
summary: He explains that after his draft notice ordered him to report in September 1951, an uncle who had served as a ground soldier in the 92nd Infantry Division and other combat veterans warned him away from the Army, so he procrastinated, then volunteered with the downtown Air Force recruiter and shipped out the next morning.

title: You Know You're a Draft Dodger
summary: Dahmer tells of being called over the loudspeaker during drill at Sheppard Air Force Base and confronted by two federal investigators who flashed badges and accused him of being a draft dodger for an induction notice he had never signed for, and how the twenty-two-year-old talked his way out of it on his feet.

title: The Best Decision Somebody Else Made
summary: He recounts being assigned to teletype school at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, resenting the typewriter and angling through the first sergeant to transfer to truck-driving school, until the captain refused and ordered him to become the best teletype operator in the outfit, a posting that carried him through communications to computers and the rank of senior master sergeant.

title: Watching the Russians From Shemya
topic: Settling Into an Air Force Career
summary: Dahmer describes marrying a month before entering the Air Force, a radar posting in Omaha, a year on isolated Shemya in the Aleutians watching Soviet aircraft, and a base near home in Panama City, Florida, where his World War II warrant officer boss and a top sergeant called him crazy for thinking of leaving, so he reenlisted for six years and was paid a stack of twenty-dollar bills that made him feel rich.

title: A Telephone Call Before Dawn
summary: Dahmer recounts being stationed at March Air Force Base in California as a master sergeant, having seen the high racial tension on a 1964 visit home, when on the morning of January 10, 1966 an aunt in Compton telephoned before dawn to tell him the family home had been firebombed.

title: You Doubt the Integrity of the FBI
topic: Going to the FBI in Riverside
summary: He describes deciding, out of distrust of Mississippi's justice system, to go to the FBI office in the Riverside federal building before work and ask that no Mississippi law enforcement handle his father's case, an agent demanding whether he doubted the FBI's integrity, and his own top-secret clearance and sensitive communications post drawing surveillance.

title: Somebody on Me the Whole Way
summary: Dahmer recounts being warned by his colonel not to compromise his position, traveling home through New Orleans, and noticing only later the same man shadowing him from Los Angeles to Mississippi, while messages tracking his own activity passed through the base facility he commanded without anyone realizing he would see them.

title: Seventy-Eight Years on Active Duty
topic: A Country That Would Not Protect Him
summary: He recounts that his father raised eight children and that seven of his sons served a combined seventy-eight years of active duty during the Korean and Vietnam eras, his parents sleeping in shifts for years, and his arrival home to the burned site and the morgue, the worst day of his life, as the oldest son shouldering the burden.

title: My Country Could Not Protect My Family
summary: Dahmer reflects that he and his brothers lived up to their military oath while the people sworn to protect his family did not, that he loved his country yet faulted it because the killing did not have to happen, and names the four sons who came home in uniform to bury their father while another, Harold, had been in the house that night.

title: She Held Everything Intact
topic: A Mother Who Rebuilt and Stayed
summary: Dahmer honors his stepmother as the leader of the family who, with two children to raise, declined relatives' offers to move to Washington, rebuilt the house on the same spot, raised Dennis Dahmer and Betty Dahmer to college degrees, served three terms as election commissioner in the district where his father was killed, and testified many times as the fourteen indicted Klansmen were repeatedly tried in the 1960s without a final conviction.

title: You Can Kill a Black Male
topic: Sam Bowers and Justice at Last
summary: Dahmer identifies Sam Bowers as the leader of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, said by former Klansmen to wield power equal to the governor's and to have told members they could kill a Black man without fear of jail, and describes leading the family effort to bring Bowers back into the courtroom, his conviction by jury verdict on August 21, 1998, his death in prison in November 2006, and the yearly January 10th memorial services and Hub City Professional Men's Club that kept the case alive across thirty-two years.

title: This Is the Burned-Out Site
summary: Dahmer closes by describing photographs for the record: his father Vernon Dahmer Sr. as a young man, the home site destroyed by the firebombing carried out by the White Knights led by Sam Bowers, the burned-out family home and grocery store with his brothers in uniform standing over the debris, and the family at the funeral.
